name: Antiquity
approximate_range: early recorded mathematics to c. 500 CE
representation_forms: numerals, units, tables, diagrams, worked examples
interpretation_note: mathematical thinking grows from counting, measurement, administration, astronomy, and construction

name: Classical deductive traditions
interpretation_note: proof becomes a central form of mathematical authority

name: Classical to medieval global traditions
representation_forms: rules, tables, commentaries, diagrams, positional numeration
interpretation_note: mathematical traditions develop across Indian, Chinese, Islamic, and other scholarly worlds

name: Medieval mathematical cultures
representation_forms: manuscripts, tables, diagrams, disputation, equation classifications
interpretation_note: mathematics is preserved, translated, taught, applied, and reorganized

name: Renaissance mathematics
representation_forms: printed texts, cossic notation, diagrams, technical manuals
interpretation_note: mathematics becomes more portable through print, symbol, commerce, navigation, perspective, and engineering

name: Early modern mathematics
interpretation_note: mathematics becomes a central language of natural science and change

name: Nineteenth-century rigor and foundations
interpretation_note: definitions, rigor, foundations, and alternative axiom systems transform mathematical thought

name: Twentieth-century structure and computation
representation_forms: formal languages, structures, graphs, matrices, algorithms
interpretation_note: mathematics increasingly studies structures, formal systems, and computational procedures

name: Contemporary computational and formal mathematics
dominant_mathematical_mode: formal verification, computation, data, simulation, proof assistants
representation_forms: code, proof scripts, formal libraries, data structures, models
interpretation_note: mathematics becomes executable, machine-checkable, and infrastructural while remaining humanly interpreted
